content.text: Why Cross-Team Dependencies Are the Top Delivery Risk

In GenAI programmes, work rarely lives inside a single team. A model training initiative depends on a data pipeline from Data Engineering. That pipeline depends on a schema approval from Security. The product feature that ships the model depends on an inference cluster from Platform Engineering. When these dependencies are undocumented, teams plan in isolation, discover blockers mid-sprint, and slide delivery dates without understanding why.
An undocumented dependency is not absent risk — it is hidden risk. The registry makes dependencies visible so they can be managed before they become blockers.
Dependency Graph

The graph shows the dependency structure for a single GenAI initiative. BLOCKING dependencies (solid lines) must be resolved before the downstream team can proceed. ENABLING dependencies (dashed) accelerate downstream work but are not strictly required. INFORMING dependencies (dotted) share knowledge without blocking work. Every arrow has a latest-start week — the last week by which the predecessor must deliver for the successor to stay on track.
Dependency Data Model

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import uuid

class DependencyType(Enum):
    BLOCKING  = "blocking"   # successor cannot start until predecessor delivers
    ENABLING  = "enabling"   # successor is faster/cheaper after predecessor delivers
    INFORMING = "informing"  # knowledge transfer only; no execution dependency

class DependencyStatus(Enum):
    OPEN        = "open"        # dependency exists; predecessor not yet delivered
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress" # predecessor is actively working on the deliverable
    DELIVERED   = "delivered"   # predecessor has delivered; successor unblocked
    AT_RISK     = "at_risk"     # predecessor has flagged they may miss the due date
    BLOCKED     = "blocked"     # predecessor is blocked by something else (cascade risk)
    CANCELLED   = "cancelled"   # dependency no longer exists

@dataclass
class TeamDependency:
    dep_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: f"DEP-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:6].upper()}")
    initiative_id: str = ""         # e.g. "GEN-A1F2" from the intake portal
    predecessor_team: str = ""      # team that must deliver first
    predecessor_deliverable: str = ""  # what specifically they must deliver
    successor_team: str = ""        # team that is waiting
    successor_blocked_work: str = ""   # what the successor cannot do until delivered
    dependency_type: DependencyType = DependencyType.BLOCKING
    latest_start_week: int = 0      # Q week by which predecessor must deliver
    due_date: Optional[date] = None # calendar date equivalent
    status: DependencyStatus = DependencyStatus.OPEN
    escalated: bool = False
    escalation_owner: Optional[str] = None
    notes: str = ""

    def is_overdue(self, current_week: int) -> bool:
        return (
            self.status not in (DependencyStatus.DELIVERED, DependencyStatus.CANCELLED)
            and current_week > self.latest_start_week
        )

    def risk_level(self, current_week: int) -> str:
        if self.status == DependencyStatus.DELIVERED:
            return "resolved"
        if self.status == DependencyStatus.BLOCKED:
            return "critical"  # cascade risk: this dep is itself blocked
        weeks_remaining = self.latest_start_week - current_week
        if self.status == DependencyStatus.AT_RISK:
            return "high"
        if weeks_remaining <= 1:
            return "high"
        if weeks_remaining <= 3:
            return "medium"
        return "low"
Registry Operations
Registering a new dependency

Dependencies should be registered during the initiative planning phase, not discovered mid-delivery. The intake and triage process (covered in a preceding topic) asks requestors to list integration dependencies — these become the seed records in the registry. Teams then refine them during sprint zero or kickoff.

import sqlite3
from datetime import date

def create_registry_schema(conn: sqlite3.Connection) -> None:
    conn.execute("""
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dependency_registry (
            dep_id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
            initiative_id       TEXT NOT NULL,
            predecessor_team    TEXT NOT NULL,
            predecessor_deliverable TEXT NOT NULL,
            successor_team      TEXT NOT NULL,
            successor_blocked_work  TEXT NOT NULL,
            dependency_type     TEXT NOT NULL,
            latest_start_week   INTEGER NOT NULL,
            due_date            TEXT,
            status              TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'open',
            escalated           INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
            escalation_owner    TEXT,
            notes               TEXT,
            created_at          TEXT NOT NULL,
            updated_at          TEXT NOT NULL
        )
    """)
    conn.commit()

def register_dependency(conn: sqlite3.Connection, dep: TeamDependency) -> None:
    now = date.today().isoformat()
    conn.execute(
        """INSERT INTO dependency_registry
           (dep_id, initiative_id, predecessor_team, predecessor_deliverable,
            successor_team, successor_blocked_work, dependency_type,
            latest_start_week, due_date, status, escalated, escalation_owner,
            notes, created_at, updated_at)
           VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)""",
        (dep.dep_id, dep.initiative_id, dep.predecessor_team,
         dep.predecessor_deliverable, dep.successor_team, dep.successor_blocked_work,
         dep.dependency_type.value, dep.latest_start_week,
         dep.due_date.isoformat() if dep.due_date else None,
         dep.status.value, int(dep.escalated), dep.escalation_owner,
         dep.notes, now, now),
    )
    conn.commit()
Updating dependency status

def update_dependency_status(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    dep_id: str,
    new_status: DependencyStatus,
    notes: str = "",
) -> None:
    conn.execute(
        """UPDATE dependency_registry
           SET status = ?, notes = notes || char(10) || ?, updated_at = ?
           WHERE dep_id = ?""",
        (new_status.value, notes, date.today().isoformat(), dep_id),
    )
    conn.commit()

def escalate_dependency(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    dep_id: str,
    escalation_owner: str,
    reason: str,
) -> None:
    """Mark a dependency as escalated and assign an owner to resolve it."""
    conn.execute(
        """UPDATE dependency_registry
           SET escalated = 1, escalation_owner = ?,
               notes = notes || char(10) || 'ESCALATED: ' || ?,
               updated_at = ?
           WHERE dep_id = ?""",
        (escalation_owner, reason, date.today().isoformat(), dep_id),
    )
    conn.commit()
Dependency Risk Report

The registry's primary operational output is a weekly risk report that surfaces dependencies likely to block delivery. The report is generated automatically and shared with team leads before each weekly status meeting.

@dataclass
class DependencyRiskItem:
    dep_id: str
    initiative_id: str
    predecessor_team: str
    successor_team: str
    deliverable: str
    risk_level: str          # "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low" | "resolved"
    weeks_overdue: int       # 0 if not yet overdue
    latest_start_week: int
    current_status: str
    escalated: bool

def build_risk_report(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    current_week: int,
) -> list[DependencyRiskItem]:
    rows = conn.execute(
        """SELECT dep_id, initiative_id, predecessor_team, successor_team,
                  predecessor_deliverable, latest_start_week, status, escalated
           FROM dependency_registry
           WHERE status NOT IN ('delivered', 'cancelled')
           ORDER BY latest_start_week ASC""",
    ).fetchall()

    items = []
    for row in rows:
        dep_id, init_id, pred, succ, deliv, lsw, status, esc = row
        # Reconstruct risk level
        weeks_over = max(0, current_week - lsw)
        if status == "blocked":
            risk = "critical"
        elif status == "at_risk" or weeks_over > 0:
            risk = "high"
        elif lsw - current_week <= 1:
            risk = "high"
        elif lsw - current_week <= 3:
            risk = "medium"
        else:
            risk = "low"
        items.append(DependencyRiskItem(
            dep_id=dep_id, initiative_id=init_id,
            predecessor_team=pred, successor_team=succ,
            deliverable=deliv, risk_level=risk,
            weeks_overdue=weeks_over, latest_start_week=lsw,
            current_status=status, escalated=bool(esc),
        ))
    return sorted(items, key=lambda x: ["critical","high","medium","low","resolved"].index(x.risk_level))
Dependency Review Meeting
Cross-team dependencies are reviewed in a standing 30-minute meeting every two weeks, attended by team leads (not their full teams). The agenda is fixed:
New dependencies (5 min)
: any dependencies registered since the last meeting. Predecessor team confirms scope and latest-start week.
Status updates (10 min)
: predecessor teams report on IN_PROGRESS items. If a team cannot commit to the latest-start week, status moves to AT_RISK immediately.
Risk items (10 min)
: CRITICAL and HIGH items only. Identify root cause, assign escalation owner, agree resolution path.
Closed dependencies (5 min)
: confirm DELIVERED items; notify successor teams they are unblocked.

The meeting does not exist to have conversations about dependency content — predecessor and successor teams do that bilaterally. The meeting exists to surface risk, make escalation decisions, and keep the registry current.
Dependency Patterns Specific to GenAI
Dependency type
Common in GenAI programmes
Typical latest-start week
Data pipeline readiness
Model training blocked until labelled data flows
W3–W4 of the quarter
Infrastructure provisioning
GPU/inference cluster needed before model deployment
W4–W6
Security / model risk review
Regulated sectors require approval before production
W6–W8 (long SLA)
Evaluation framework
Fine-tuning team needs eval harness before comparing runs
W2–W3
API schema agreement
Product team building UI before model API is stable
W5–W7
Vendor contract / procurement
New LLM provider needs legal sign-off before use
W1 (must start immediately)
Prompt engineering handoff
Platform team needs validated prompts before gateway config
W4–W6
Cascade Risk — When Dependencies Have Dependencies

A GenAI programme can have chains: Data Engineering depends on a vendor data feed; ML Engineering depends on Data Engineering; Product depends on ML Engineering. If the vendor feed slips, all three downstream teams are at risk simultaneously. The registry must model these chains to detect cascade risk.

from collections import defaultdict, deque

def find_cascade_risk(
    conn: sqlite3.Connection,
    blocked_dep_id: str,
) -> list[str]:
    """
    Given a dependency that is BLOCKED or AT_RISK, return all dep_ids
    that are transitively at risk (BFS through successor relationships).
    """
    # Build adjacency: deliverable → deps that consume it
    all_deps = conn.execute(
        "SELECT dep_id, predecessor_deliverable, successor_team FROM dependency_registry "
        "WHERE status NOT IN ('delivered', 'cancelled')"
    ).fetchall()

    # Map predecessor_deliverable → list of dep_ids that are downstream
    downstream: dict[str, list[str]] = defaultdict(list)
    dep_deliverable = {}
    dep_successor = {}
    for dep_id, deliv, succ in all_deps:
        dep_deliverable[dep_id] = deliv
        dep_successor[dep_id] = succ
        downstream[deliv].append(dep_id)

    # BFS from the blocked dependency
    visited = set()
    queue = deque([blocked_dep_id])
    while queue:
        current = queue.popleft()
        if current in visited:
            continue
        visited.add(current)
        succ_team = dep_successor.get(current, "")
        # Find deps where this successor team is the predecessor
        for dep_id, deliv, succ in all_deps:
            if succ_team and dep_id not in visited:
                queue.append(dep_id)

    return [d for d in visited if d != blocked_dep_id]
Integration with the Portfolio and OKR Systems
The dependency registry is most powerful when connected to the portfolio plan and OKR system. Three integration points create a closed loop:
Portfolio plan integration

When a project enters the committed portfolio plan, its blocking dependencies are imported into the registry automatically. The portfolio planner checks that all BLOCKING dependencies have a latest-start week earlier than the successor project's planned start week — if not, the sequencing is flagged as infeasible before the plan is committed.
OKR integration

If a BLOCKING dependency is marked AT_RISK or BLOCKED, and the successor team has a key result that depends on that deliverable, the OKR system should automatically flag the affected KR as AT_RISK. This prevents teams from unknowingly reporting a KR as ON_TRACK when an upstream dependency has already failed.
Retrospective feed

At quarter close, the registry produces a dependency retrospective: how many blocking dependencies were resolved on time vs late, which predecessor teams were consistently reliable, and which dependency types caused the most delays. This data informs both next-quarter planning (more generous latest-start weeks for historically unreliable dependency types) and team maturity conversations.
Summary

A cross-team dependency registry makes the invisible visible: every deliverable that one team needs from another is recorded with a type (blocking / enabling / informing), a latest-start week, and a current status. The registry is populated during initiative planning, updated weekly by predecessor teams, and reviewed in a standing biweekly meeting. A weekly automated risk report flags CRITICAL and HIGH items before they become sprint blockers. Cascade risk analysis — tracing chains of dependencies through the graph — is especially important in GenAI programmes where data pipelines, inference infrastructure, security reviews, and product integrations form long sequential chains. The cost of running the registry is low; the cost of not running it is measured in sprint delays and missed quarter commitments.
